response:
1. Deriving the total transformation matrix \( T \) requires combining the rotational and translational components. Each spin is a rotation of \( \theta = 2\pi \) radians. The rotation matrix \( R(2\pi) \) is the identity matrix \( I \), as rotating a full circle returns the object to its original orientation. Performing three such spins results in \( R(2\pi)^3 = I^3 = I \). Consequently, the cumulative rotational effect after the three spins is neutral.

The leaps are modeled as vertical displacements \( \vec{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ h \end{pmatrix} \). Since the rotation is the identity, the direction of the leap vector remains unchanged by the preceding spins. The problem states each leap follows the cumulative effect of the spins, meaning the position updates sequentially. In a 2D affine transformation matrix (augmented to 3x3 to handle translation), a pure vertical translation by \( h \) is represented as:
\[ T_{leap} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & h \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \]
Two consecutive leaps result in a total vertical displacement of \( 2h \). Since the rotation matrices are all identity matrices, the total transformation matrix \( T \) for the sequence (3 spins + 2 leaps) is simply the product of the identity matrices and the two translation matrices:
\[ T = T_{leap} \cdot T_{leap} \cdot I \cdot I \cdot I = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & h \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & h \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 2h \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \]
Thus, the total transformation matrix is \( \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 2h \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \).
